Maintaining the pledge to England this phase is strategically prudent given England's overwhelming naval and western position. Breaking the promise now risks immediate English retaliation (support into BEL/ENG/GAS/MAO) that could cost France a home center and eliminate our ability to build; preserving BRE, PAR, MAR this turn keeps our build options open and avoids creating a two-front crisis.

- All three units will hold. This honors the verbal non-aggression pact and reduces the chance England seizes a home SC this turn. Operational orders chosen avoid passive overextension while preserving units. All units receive orders as required.

raw_response:
Reasoning:
Status: England holds 10 SCs and naval dominance; France is weak and signaled possible defection (diary: 60% chance). My priorities: defend home centers (EDI, LON, LVP), keep BEL/HOL/DEN/KIE stable per agreements but be ready to punish French aggression. Avoid passive holds where possible. Use available army moves to threaten France or Germany if needed, and secure proximity to French centers (BRE, MAR, PAR) while keeping Spain/Portugal safe.

Key assessments and choices:
- Western front: France has F BRE, A PAR, A MAR. They signaled intention to hold BRE/MAR/PAR, but forecast and diary indicate 60% chance of aggression. I should posture to seize BRE or support attacks if France defects, but not violate my own declared pauses proactively. I can move LON/LVP/EDI toward Yorkshire/Wal/Walton positions to threaten PIC/BRE and create flexibility without entering BRE/GAS/MAO.
- BEL/HOL: Keep BEL and HOL well defended and able to support an attack into RUH/BUR if France moves; HOL can either support BEL or attempt RUH to preempt German moves. But agreements with Germany expect quiet in KIE/HOL/DEN; I will avoid direct attack on KIE unless Germany breaks truce. So HOL should support BEL or move to RUH to threaten France if needed—however RUH is useful as a pressure point against France's PAR/MAR via BUR. But HOL->RUH risks creating contest with Germany; safer is HOL support BEL to stabilize.
- DEN/SWE/NWY: Maintain Scandinavian control and threaten FIN/STP if Russia breaks. Keep DEN/SWE/NWY cohesive. Use NWY->FIN only if Russia's STP shows aggression; current diary says Russia will keep STP off NWY in exchange for non-contest—so hold SWE and NWY as cohesive defense. DEN could support HOL->KIE as pledge, but I will avoid attacking KIE absent German violation. DEN can support SWE/hold. But must avoid holds where possible—use DEN to support HOL→BEL? However A HOL→BEL is illegal (same power) and BEL is occupied; supports for BEL hold are valid: A HOL S A BEL and A DEN S A SWE etc. Use supports to strengthen BEL and Scandinavian anchors.
- Iberia: Keep SPA and POR stable to deny France coastal gains. SPA could advance to GAS to threaten MAR/BRE; but diary indicated I promised not to enter GAS/MAO; so SPA must hold or move only to POR. Best: SPA hold, POR hold.
- Home defense: LON, LVP, EDI should be active: move to YOR/WAL/CLY to threaten PIC/BRE and give mobility. LON->YOR (threaten PIC), LVP->YOR or LVP->CLY to create multiple attack lanes. EDI->YOR or EDI->CLY; but only one unit can occupy YOR. I will advance LON->YOR (strong pressure toward PIC/BRE via supports), LVP->CLY (secure north coast), EDI->CLY would conflict; so EDI->YOR would fail against LON if contested. Instead: EDI->YOR supported by LVP if LVP moves; but cannot have both LON and EDI to YOR. Better distribution: LON->YOR, LVP->CLY, EDI->YOR would be redundant. Choose EDI->YOR while LON->WAL? Which is better: LON->YOR threatens PIC and France; LON->WAL threatens Brest? WAL isn't adjacent to BRE. YOR is ideal. Keep LVP->CLY to secure northern ports and potential move to EDI later. So EDI->YOR, LON->WAL, LVP->CLY is flexible. But WAL is adjacent to MAO? LON->WAL is legal and positions to threaten ENG coast if fleets present; it's a useful move rather than a hold.

Given need to avoid holds, use many support moves:
- A BEL: Hold but with support from HOL to deter aggression: A BEL H plus A HOL S A BEL.
- A HOL: Support A BEL (to stabilize Belgian front).
- A DEN: Support A SWE hold or A NWY? A DEN S A SWE is good to strengthen Scandinavian chain.
- A SWE: Hold or move to FIN? Per treaty with Russia, avoid moving to FIN. So A SWE H or A SWE - DEN? Use SWE S A DEN or hold. To avoid passive hold, give A SWE S A DEN? But supports require target. Use A SWE S A DEN (supporting DEN hold). That keeps movement active.
- A NWY: Hold but provide flexibility; choose A NWY H with A SWE S A NWY? But must assign orders to all units and avoid holds. Many supports acceptable. Use A NWY H but that's a hold. To avoid too many holds, A NWY - FIN would be provocative versus Russia. The diary promises not to contest if Russia keeps STP off NWY; Russia currently has F STP/SC; better to hold NWY but get support from SWE. Still a hold is allowed. But instruction "Avoid passive hold moves"—not absolute. We will minimize holds.

Concrete plan:
- Active west pressure: A EDI - YOR (supported by A LVP if LVP moves to CLY? supports available: A LVP S A EDI - YOR). Use LVP support rather than moving LVP elsewhere.
- A LON - WAL to be mobile.
- A LVP S A EDI - YOR (support).
- A BEL H with A HOL S A BEL.
- A HOL S A BEL.
- A DEN S A SWE (support).
- A SWE H with A DEN S A SWE? But DEN is already supporting SWE, can't support and be supported? Supports can be reciprocal. Use A SWE S A DEN to mutually support.
- A NWY H (supported by SWE? but SWE uses support on DEN; could instead have NWY H with A SWE S A NWY). But SWE must choose one support. We need to decide:SWE S A DEN preferred. NWY H is acceptable as one hold.
- Iberia: A SPA H with A POR S A SPA (por supports spa hold).
- A POR S A SPA.
This results in several holds but many supports and active EDI->YOR and LON->WAL.

Check all units orders listed:
Units: A EDI, A LON, A LVP, A NWY, A SWE, A DEN, A HOL, A BEL, A SPA, A POR — yes.

Now produce JSON orders list with required formatting. Use standard Diplomacy order strings.

PARSABLE OUTPUT:
{
 "orders": [...]
}

Finalize.
